sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=20).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 70, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 38 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good - clicking (17, 20) the top-middle red block also works as a trigger. The strip shrunk from 64 to 63 (1px), same as before. The game is no longer "over" - it reset after the game over state.
Now I need to figure out the actual completion condition. The blind rotation approach failed because the board cycles back after 61 rotations without the strip reaching 0.
Let me think about what the level might actually require. Looking at the colored blocks, they seem to be arranged in specific patterns. Maybe the goal is to rotate until the blocks form a specific configuration.
Looking at the current image more carefully:
- There are colored blocks arranged in what looks like a pattern
- Yellow markers at certain positions
- The blocks seem to be in rows
Wait, let me reconsider the problem. The "about 38 target actions" hint suggests the level should be solvable in about 38 actions. If the rotation period is 61, and I need to reach a specific configuration, maybe the target is at rotation 23 (since 61-23=38, meaning 23 rotations in one direction equals 38 in the other).
But I already tried rotating 38 times with (25, 13) and the level didn't complete. However, I was clicking (25, 13) which is the middle-left red block. Now I'm clicking (17, 20) which is the top-middle red block. Maybe these two red blocks rotate in different directions or affect different sets of blocks!
Let me check: when I click (25, 13), do the blocks rotate clockwise or counterclockwise? And when I click (17, 20), do they rotate in the same or opposite direction?
If they rotate in opposite directions, then clicking one "undoes" the other. This would explain why the period is 61 - maybe there are two independent rotation systems.
Let me test this hypothesis by clicking (25, 13) once and seeing if it reverses the effect of clicking (17, 20).
Actually, let me first look at what changed when I clicked (17, 20). The strip went from 64 to 63. Let me see what the colored blocks look like now compared to the initial state.
From the images:
- Initial state (before any clicks on this reset): blocks in certain positions
- After clicking (17, 20): blocks shifted
Let me compare the current frame to the initial frame to see which blocks moved. But I don't have easy access to the initial frame anymore since the history is long.
Let me try a different approach: click (17, 20) a few more times and see if the level completes at some point. Given the "about 38" target, let me try rotating with this trigger and see if the completion condition is met earlier.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The key insight might be that there are TWO red blocks that rotate in OPPOSITE directions. If I click one, blocks go clockwise; if I click the other, blocks go counterclockwise. The level might require reaching a specific configuration that's closer in one direction than the other.
Let me test: click (17, 20) a few times, then click (25, 13) a few times, and see if the strip behavior changes or if the blocks move differently.
Wait, but the strip shrinks regardless of which red block I click. This suggests both triggers contribute to the same "progress" metric. But maybe they rotate blocks in different directions.
Let me try a quick test: click (17, 20) twice, then click (25, 13) twice, and see if the board returns to the state after just 2 rotations with (17, 20).
Actually, I should be more careful. Let me first understand the current state better. I've clicked (17, 20) once, and the strip is at 63px. Let me click it a few more times and monitor the strip and block positions.
Given the efficiency concern, let me try a batch of rotations with (17, 20) and see if the level completes at some point. If the target is around 38 actions total (and I've already used 62), I need to be very careful.
But wait - the game reset after the "game over" state, so maybe the action counter also reset? Let me check the step count: step 70, level 2. The step count is cumulative across the run, not per level.
Let me just try rotating with (17, 20) and see what happens. I'll do a small batch first to understand the behavior.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Clicked (17,20) once, strip went 64->63. Game not over.
# Let me test if (17,20) and (25,13) rotate in opposite directions.
# First, record current block positions, then click (25,13) once.

# Get current colored block positions (non-background, non-HUD)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
# Get all small colored blocks (2x2 = 4 pixels)
small_blocks = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ('N', 'B', 'g', 'G')]
print(f"Small colored blocks: {len(small_blocks)}")
for n in small_blocks:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    print(f"  {n['color']} at {center}, hash={n['hash'][:8]}")

# Now click (25,13) once to see if it reverses
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 13}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
small_blocks2 = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ('N', 'B', 'g', 'G')]
print(f"\nAfter (25,13): {len(small_blocks2)} blocks")
for n in small_blocks2:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    print(f"  {n['color']} at {center}, hash={n['hash'][:8]}")

# Check strip
green_nodes = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"Green strip: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: Both red blocks (17,20) and (25,13) trigger rotations. Strip shrinks ~1px/rot. Period=61.", "object_taxonomy": "Red=trigger, Green strip=progress bar, colored blocks=cyclically rotating", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks ~1px/rotation in Level 2", "win_pattern": "Unknown - not strip depletion", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rotations. Level 2: failed with 61 rotations, reset, testing different triggers.", "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 may have multiple triggers with different effects"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 2 has 3 red triggers. Testing if they rotate in different directions.", "goal_model": "Find correct trigger sequence to complete level", "recent_findings": ["Clicking (17,20) works, strip shrinks 1px", "Game reset after game_over state"], "open_questions": ["Do different red blocks rotate in opposite directions?", "What is the completion condition?"], "current_plan": "Test if (25,13) reverses (17,20) rotation", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
